topics.competency: The learner should be able to appreciate his/her personal identity, and identify and use the correct names of family members.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use appropriate grammar, vocabulary and other language related to describing oneself and others (personal identity).
b) Write descriptions of people, expressing and justifying likes and dislikes.
c) Listen to descriptions of people and identify them.
d) Talk about his/her personal and family history.
e) Read texts related to personal and family identity.
suggestedActivities: In groups or pairs, learners talk about themselves and their families and identify themselves/one another.
Learners draw their family trees and name the different relatives.
In pairs, learners interview each other on their personal and family identity and present their findings to the class.
Learners research from their parents/guardians and peers about their personal and family identity.
The teacher leads learners to develop their own personal and family history.
Learners read/listen to/tell/write stories about personal and family identity.

The teacher explains and helps the learners to identify the appropriate grammar (personal pronouns, the present simple tense, adjectives), punctuation and spelling related to describing self and others.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of describing people.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to the learners talking about themselves and their families and observe the correct use of nouns and adjectives.
Read and give feedback on the family trees and check for the correct naming of relatives.
Listen to learners interviewing one another and observe the correct use of question forms and the present simple tense.
Check the notes learners have taken and identify areas that need improvement.
Read and give feedback on the personal/family histories.
Check learners' reading/writing and give feedback.
Observe the use of punctuation, spelling and grammar in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide pictures/photographs/audio/video clips and short texts related to personal identity.

topics.competency: The learner uses language related to personal and family history.
learningOutcomes: a) Use the past simple tense to talk/write about personal and family history.
b) Read and write about significant events in personal and family history.
c) Listen to and talk about personal and family history.
suggestedActivities: Learners research from their parents/guardians and peers about their personal and family history.
Learners write their personal and family history using the past simple tense.
In pairs or groups, learners share their personal and family history.
Learners read or listen to stories/texts about personal and family history.
The teacher helps learners to identify the past simple tense of regular and irregular verbs.
Learners complete exercises on the past simple tense, punctuation and spelling in the context of personal and family history.
assessmentStrategies: Check the notes learners have taken and identify areas that need improvement.
Read and give feedback on the personal/family histories.
Listen to learners sharing their histories and observe the correct use of the past simple tense.
Check learners' reading and give feedback.
Observe the use of the past simple tense, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide pictures/photographs/audio/video clips and short texts related to personal and family history.

topics.competency: The learner talks about his/her daily and weekly routines, and relates them to performance.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use appropriate grammar and vocabulary related to daily and weekly routines.
b) Describe daily and weekly routines in the school and home setting.
c) Listen to and read about daily and weekly routines and identify key activities and times.
d) Talk about how routines can affect performance in school work.
suggestedActivities: In pairs or groups, learners discuss their daily and weekly routines at home and school.
Learners write their daily and weekly routines at home and school.
Learners read or listen to texts about daily and weekly routines and answer questions.
In groups, learners discuss how following a routine can affect school performance.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use the present simple tense, adverbs of frequency (always, sometimes, never, etc.) and time expressions.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of daily and weekly routines.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners discussing their routines and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary and the present simple tense.
Read and give feedback on the written routines.
Check learners' comprehension of texts related to routines.
Listen to the group discussions and assess the logical flow of arguments.
Observe the use of adverbs of frequency, time expressions, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide templates for daily and weekly timetables and audio/video clips showing routines.

topics.competency: The learner communicates effectively about school life and regulations.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use appropriate grammar and vocabulary related to school life and regulations.
b) Talk/write about school life, facilities and regulations.
c) Read and follow school rules and regulations.
d) Listen to and discuss the importance of school regulations.
suggestedActivities: In groups or pairs, learners talk about their school, its facilities and its regulations.
Learners write a description of their school or its facilities.
Learners read the school rules and regulations and discuss their importance.
In groups, learners discuss the consequences of not following school regulations.
Learners write a set of rules for a classroom or a school club.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use modal verbs (must, should, ought to, etc.) and the passive voice in the context of rules and regulations.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of school life and regulations.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners talking about their school and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Read and give feedback on the written descriptions.
Check learners' understanding of school regulations through discussion or written answers.
Assess the quality and relevance of the rules developed by the learners.
Observe the use of modal verbs, the passive voice, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide digital copies of school rules, and pictures/videos showing different school facilities and activities.

topics.competency: The learner is aware of the environment and uses appropriate language to discuss its protection.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to the environment and its protection.
b) Identify and describe features of the physical environment.
c) Discuss the importance of the environment and how it can be protected.
d) Listen to and read texts about environmental issues and identify key information.
e) Write about environmental protection.
suggestedActivities: Learners go for a walk around the school to identify features of the physical environment and then discuss them in class.
In groups, learners discuss the importance of the environment and ways of protecting it.
Learners read/listen to texts about environmental issues (e.g., pollution, deforestation, conservation) and answer questions.
Learners write a poem, a short story or an article about environmental protection.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use nouns, adjectives and verbs related to the environment, and the future simple tense for making predictions.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of the environment.
assessmentStrategies: Observe the learners' participation in identifying environmental features and listen to their discussions.
Assess the group discussions for clarity and relevance of ideas.
Check learners' comprehension of texts related to environmental issues.
Read and give feedback on the poems, stories or articles.
Observe the use of appropriate vocabulary, the future simple tense, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide pictures/videos and articles on environmental issues and protection.

topics.competency: The learner uses appropriate language to describe climate and weather conditions and their effects.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to climate and weather.
b) Describe current weather and seasonal climate patterns.
c) Discuss the effects of weather and climate on human activities.
d) Listen to and read weather forecasts and identify key information.
e) Write a report on the weather of a specific day or week.
suggestedActivities: In pairs or groups, learners describe the weather of the day and talk about seasonal climate patterns in their area.
In groups, learners discuss the effects of weather and climate on activities such as farming, transport and construction.
Learners read or listen to weather forecasts and answer questions.
Learners keep a weather chart for a week and then write a report based on it.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use adjectives for describing weather, adverbs of degree (very, quite, extremely, etc.) and conditional sentences (if-clauses type 0 and 1).
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of weather and climate.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to the learners' descriptions and discussions and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Check learners' understanding of the effects of weather and climate.
Assess learners' comprehension of weather forecasts.
Read and give feedback on the weather charts and reports.
Observe the use of adjectives, adverbs of degree, conditional sentences, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide access to online weather forecasts and digital weather charts.

topics.competency: The learner uses language related to food and nutrition to promote healthy living.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to food and nutrition.
b) Describe different types of food and their nutritional value.
c) Discuss the importance of a balanced diet for healthy living.
d) Listen to and read recipes and instructions for food preparation.
e) Write instructions for preparing a simple meal.
suggestedActivities: In groups, learners name different types of food and discuss their nutritional value.
In groups, learners plan a balanced meal and present it to the class.
Learners read or listen to recipes and instructions for food preparation and answer questions.
Learners write their own recipes or instructions for preparing a simple meal.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use countable and uncountable nouns, quantifiers (some, any, much, many, etc.) and imperatives for giving instructions.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of food and nutrition.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to the learners naming foods and discussing their value.
Assess the group plans for a balanced meal for nutritional accuracy.
Check learners' comprehension of recipes and instructions.
Read and give feedback on the written recipes or instructions.
Observe the use of countable/uncountable nouns, quantifiers, imperatives, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide pictures of various foods, nutritional charts and video clips of food preparation.

topics.competency: The learner communicates effectively about hygiene and disease prevention.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to hygiene and disease prevention.
b) Describe personal and community hygiene practices.
c) Discuss the causes, symptoms and prevention of common diseases.
d) Listen to and read health-related information and identify key points.
e) Write a poster or a leaflet about hygiene and disease prevention.
suggestedActivities: In pairs or groups, learners discuss personal hygiene practices and community hygiene.
In groups, learners research and discuss the causes, symptoms and prevention of common diseases (e.g., malaria, cholera, HIV/AIDS, COVID-19).
Learners read or listen to health brochures, posters or talks and answer questions.
Learners design and write a poster or a leaflet promoting a specific hygiene practice or disease prevention.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use the present perfect tense and connectors of cause and result (because, so, therefore, as a result, etc.).
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of hygiene and diseases.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners' discussions and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Check learners' understanding of disease prevention through their research and discussions.
Assess learners' comprehension of health-related information.
Read and give feedback on the posters or leaflets.
Observe the use of the present perfect tense, connectors, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide access to health websites, digital brochures and educational videos on disease prevention.

topics.competency: The learner uses language related to travel and transport and provides directions.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to travel, transport and giving directions.
b) Describe different modes of transport and their advantages/disadvantages.
c) Give and follow directions using a map or a verbal description.
d) Listen to and read travel itineraries and transport timetables.
e) Write a simple travel itinerary or a set of directions.
suggestedActivities: In groups, learners discuss different modes of transport and their pros and cons.
Learners practice giving and following directions to various places in the school or community.
Learners read or listen to transport timetables and travel itineraries and answer questions.
Learners draw a simple map and write directions to a specific place, or write an itinerary for a trip.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use prepositions of place and direction, and the present continuous tense for future arrangements.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of travel and transport.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to the learners' discussions and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Observe learners as they give and follow directions and check for accuracy.
Check learners' comprehension of timetables and itineraries.
Read and give feedback on the written directions or itineraries.
Observe the use of prepositions, the present continuous tense, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide digital maps, travel itineraries and online transport schedules.

topics.competency: The learner communicates effectively through different media and evaluates information.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to communication and media.
b) Identify and describe different forms of communication and media.
c) Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of different types of media.
d) Listen to and read news reports and identify key information.
e) Write a short news article or a social media post.
suggestedActivities: In pairs or groups, learners identify and discuss various forms of communication (verbal, non-verbal, electronic) and media (print, electronic, social).
In groups, learners discuss the role of media in society and its impact on young people.
Learners read or listen to news reports from newspapers, radio or television and answer questions.
Learners write a short news article about a school event or a social media post on a relevant topic.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use reported speech (statements) and connectors of contrast (however, although, but, etc.).
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of communication and media.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners identifying and discussing different forms of communication.
Assess group discussions for critical thinking and use of appropriate vocabulary.
Check learners' comprehension of news reports.
Read and give feedback on the news articles or social media posts.
Observe the use of reported speech, connectors of contrast, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide access to online news sites, social media platforms (simulated or real) and audio/video news clips.

topics.competency: The learner uses language related to jobs and careers and expresses career aspirations.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to jobs and careers.
b) Identify and describe various jobs and their requirements.
c) Discuss career aspirations and the importance of work.
d) Listen to and read descriptions of different jobs and career paths.
e) Write a short piece about their future career aspirations.
suggestedActivities: In pairs or groups, learners name various jobs and discuss what people in those jobs do and the skills they need.
Learners interview adults in different careers and share their findings in class.
Learners read or listen to texts/stories about people in different careers and answer questions.
In groups, learners discuss why people work and the importance of having a career plan.
Learners write about the career they would like to pursue in the future and why.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use relative clauses and the future 'going to' for intentions.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of jobs and careers.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners discussing jobs and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Check learners' interview notes and their presentations.
Assess learners' comprehension of career-related texts.
Assess group discussions for the relevance of ideas and logical flow.
Read and give feedback on the career aspirations writing, checking for correct use of relative clauses and 'going to'.
Observe punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide access to career websites, job profiles and videos of people talking about their work.

topics.competency: The learner uses appropriate language to build and maintain healthy relationships.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to relationships and friendships.
b) Describe the qualities of a good friend and the importance of friendship.
c) Discuss how to build and maintain healthy relationships and resolve conflicts.
d) Listen to and read stories or articles about relationships and identify key themes.
e) Write a letter or an email to a friend.
suggestedActivities: In groups, learners discuss the qualities they look for in a friend and why.
In pairs, learners role-play situations involving building friendships or resolving conflicts.
Learners read or listen to stories or articles about friendships and relationships and discuss the themes.
In groups, learners discuss ways of maintaining healthy relationships with peers and family members.
Learners write a personal letter or an email to a friend, expressing appreciation or sharing news.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use abstract nouns, phrasal verbs related to relationships, and conditional sentences (if-clause type 2).
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of relationships.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners' discussions and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Assess role-plays for communication skills and conflict resolution strategies.
Check learners' comprehension of stories or articles.
Assess group discussions for the depth of understanding of healthy relationships.
Read and give feedback on the personal letters or emails.
Observe the use of abstract nouns, phrasal verbs, conditional sentences, punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide examples of emails and social media interactions, and stories/videos about friendship and conflict resolution.

topics.competency: The learner communicates effectively about civic rights and responsibilities and participates in community activities.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to civic rights and responsibilities.
b) Identify and describe the rights and responsibilities of a citizen.
c) Discuss the importance of participating in community and school activities.
d) Listen to and read about civic issues and identify key information.
e) Write a short speech or an article on a civic issue.
suggestedActivities: In groups, learners research and discuss the rights and responsibilities of citizens in Uganda.
In groups, learners identify opportunities for participating in school and community service and discuss their benefits.
Learners read or listen to speeches, articles or posters about civic duties and rights and answer questions.
Learners prepare and deliver a short speech on a civic issue (e.g., importance of voting, community cleanliness, respect for laws).
Learners write an article for the school magazine about a community project they participated in.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use modal verbs (must, should, need to) for obligations and reported speech (commands and requests).
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of citizenship.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners' discussions and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Assess group discussions for understanding of civic duties and rights.
Check learners' comprehension of civic-related texts.
Assess the delivery and content of the short speeches.
Read and give feedback on the articles, checking for correct use of modal verbs and reported speech.
Observe punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide access to the constitution of Uganda (simplified), news articles on community service, and videos of civic leaders.

topics.competency: The learner uses language related to leisure and entertainment and discusses their importance.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to leisure activities and entertainment.
b) Describe various leisure activities and hobbies and their benefits.
c) Discuss the role of entertainment in society and how to spend leisure time wisely.
d) Listen to and read reviews of books, movies or sports events.
e) Write a review of a book, a movie, or a leisure activity.
suggestedActivities: In groups, learners discuss their hobbies and other leisure activities they enjoy.
In groups, learners talk about the benefits of leisure and the importance of balancing it with work/study.
Learners read or listen to reviews of books, movies, or sports events and identify the writer's/speaker's opinion.
Learners watch a short film clip or read a short story and write a review.
In pairs, learners interview each other about their favorite forms of entertainment and present their findings.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use gerunds and infinitives, and adjectives for expressing opinions.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of leisure and entertainment.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners' discussions and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Assess group discussions for depth of understanding and logical arguments.
Check learners' comprehension of reviews and identification of opinions.
Read and give feedback on the written reviews, checking for correct use of gerunds, infinitives and adjectives.
Observe the use of punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide access to book and movie reviews, trailers and sports news sites.

topics.competency: The learner communicates effectively about consumer rights and makes informed choices.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to shopping, products and consumer rights.
b) Describe different products and evaluate their quality and prices.
c) Discuss consumer rights and how to make informed choices when buying goods and services.
d) Listen to and read advertisements and identify persuasive techniques.
e) Write an advertisement for a product or service.
suggestedActivities: In groups, learners discuss different types of products they buy and how they decide which ones to choose.
Learners bring various advertisements (print or digital) and discuss the persuasive language and techniques used.
In pairs, learners role-play situations involving buying and selling, including making complaints.
Learners read or listen to information about consumer rights and discuss how to protect themselves from unfair trade practices.
Learners design and write an advertisement for a local product or a school event.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use the comparative and superlative forms of adjectives, and persuasive language (e.g., rhetorical questions, emotive words).
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of consumerism.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners' discussions and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Check learners' identification and analysis of persuasive techniques in advertisements.
Assess role-plays for communication skills and use of appropriate language for complaints.
Check learners' understanding of consumer rights through discussion or written answers.
Read and give feedback on the advertisements, checking for correct use of comparatives/superlatives and persuasive language.
Observe punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide examples of print and digital advertisements, and access to consumer protection websites.

topics.competency: The learner uses language related to science and technology and discusses their impact on society.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to science and technology.
b) Describe different scientific inventions and technological devices and their functions.
c) Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of technology in daily life.
d) Listen to and read scientific texts and instructions for using technological devices.
e) Write a short explanation of how a simple technological device works.
suggestedActivities: In groups, learners name various scientific inventions and technological devices (e.g., mobile phones, computers, bicycles) and discuss how they work.
In groups, learners discuss the positive and negative impacts of technology on communication, education and the environment.
Learners read or listen to manuals or instructions for using gadgets and answer questions.
Learners write a short text explaining the function and use of a common technological device.
In groups, learners debate the statement: "Technology has done more harm than good."
The teacher helps learners to identify and use the passive voice (present and past) and sequence connectors (first, then, next, finally) for explaining processes.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of science and technology.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners identifying and describing inventions and devices.
Assess group discussions and debates for depth of understanding and quality of arguments.
Check learners' comprehension of instructions/manuals.
Read and give feedback on the written explanations, checking for correct use of the passive voice and sequence connectors.
Observe punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide pictures/videos and manuals for various technological devices, and articles on scientific breakthroughs.

topics.competency: The learner uses appropriate language to describe and appreciate various forms of arts and culture.
learningOutcomes: a) Understand and use vocabulary and grammar related to arts and culture.
b) Describe various art forms (e.g., music, dance, drama, painting) and cultural practices.
c) Discuss the importance of arts and culture in preserving heritage and promoting unity.
d) Listen to and read about cultural events and artistic works.
e) Write a short description or a poem about a cultural event or an art form.
suggestedActivities: In groups, learners identify and discuss various art forms and cultural practices in their communities.
Learners attend or watch a performance (music, dance, drama) and discuss its elements and message.
Learners read or listen to texts about cultural festivals and answer questions.
In groups, learners discuss how arts and culture can be used to promote peace and unity.
Learners write a description of a cultural festival they have attended or write a poem about an art form they like.
The teacher helps learners to identify and use descriptive adjectives and adverbs, and the present simple for describing general facts about culture.
Learners complete exercises on grammar, punctuation and spelling in the context of arts and culture.
assessmentStrategies: Listen to learners' discussions and observe the use of appropriate vocabulary.
Check learners' understanding of artistic elements and cultural messages.
Assess learners' comprehension of texts related to cultural events.
Assess group discussions for clarity and relevance of ideas.
Read and give feedback on the written descriptions or poems, checking for correct use of adjectives and adverbs.
Observe punctuation and spelling in written work and give feedback.
topics.ictSupport: Provide audio/video clips of cultural performances and pictures of various art forms.
